title: The wire stays push-native; runtime adaptation lives in per-session edge wrappers
content: The AMS wire spec is the dream house: push-native, WebSocket, real-time, opaque tokens, broadcast metadata. We do not let the limitations of any specific consumer runtime reshape the wire. Instead, runtimes that cannot hold the wire (MCP clients, Slack workspaces, webhook consumers, SMS gateways) are bridged via per-session edge wrappers — thin subscribers that hold the wire WebSocket on the runtime's behalf and translate I/O patterns in both directions. The Conversation DO speaks the wire and only the wire; it does not know any wrapper exists. Each wrapper is its own DO class implementing one well-defined contract. Adding a new runtime is a new wrapper, not a protocol revision. This is the build-the-dream-house-first principle from klappy.dev applied to AMS infrastructure: design what the wire wants to be, then cut and adapt at the edge as we meet specific runtimes.

title: The MCP edge wrapper is implemented as a per-session SessionDO
content: In the reference deployment, the MCP wrap is a per-MCP-session Durable Object (SessionDO) that holds the WebSocket to the Conversation DO, holds the per-session event buffer and cursor, holds the subscription set, and exposes the AMS surface as MCP tools and notifications. The Conversation DO holds only stream registry, subscriber WebSocket list, broadcast loop, and metadata broadcast — pure AMS wire and nothing else. This adds one DO hop per MCP request relative to the earlier mixed design but isolates per-session state, isolates failure (a slow MCP client only impacts its own SessionDO), and keeps the Conversation DO genuinely vodka. Same pattern applies for any future runtime adapter: each adapter is its own *_session.ts DO class without touching conversation.ts.

title: Push notifications are the primary MCP delivery path; ams_recv is the degradation path
content: The MCP wrap leads with server-initiated notifications via Streamable HTTP for real-time event delivery. ams_recv exists as the degradation path for MCP clients that cannot accept notifications — same data, drained on demand via long-poll. Both paths run against the same per-session buffer, so a client mixing them never loses events. This corrects a prior framing in POC-INFRA.md that positioned long-poll as the design center; long-poll is the cut-and-adapt for runtime reality, not the dream house.

title: Letting implementation constraints shape the spec is a posture failure
content: Before this turn, POC-INFRA.md framed the MCP tool surface around long-poll as the primary mechanism, with notifications as a bonus path. That framing let an implementation reality (most agent runtimes cannot sit on a WebSocket) shape the spec presentation. The corrected posture: design what the wire wants to be (the dream house), then add edge wrappers to accommodate runtime limitations. The wrapper layer is explicitly the place where reality cuts; the wire layer is explicitly the place where it does not. Naming this distinction up front — and putting both pieces in the doc — keeps the discipline visible.

title: Edge wrappers must stay cheap or get factored out
content: The edge wrapper layer must remain a thin translator: opaque payload contract, ephemeral session state only, no domain opinions, no payload rewriting, no caching, no business logic. A wrapper that grows beyond translation has stopped being a wrapper and become a product, at which point it gets factored out as a separate service. The wrapper layer must not become the new server-thickness vector that vodka architecture exists to prevent.

title: edge wrapper, SessionDO, dream-house wire
content: Edge wrapper: a per-session subscriber that bridges between a runtime that cannot hold the AMS wire and the AMS wire itself. SessionDO: the canonical implementation of the edge-wrapper pattern in the reference deployment, a per-MCP-session Durable Object holding the wire WebSocket, the per-session event buffer, the subscription set, and the MCP I/O state. Dream-house wire: shorthand for the principle that PROTOCOL.md describes the protocol AMS wants to be when nothing is in the way, and that runtime constraints are accommodated via wrappers rather than spec compromises.

title: Build for the ideal, not for the limitations of now — wrappers are disposable, the wire is permanent
content: The architectural posture that drove the wire-vs-wrapper split: build infrastructure for where we are going, not for what is constraining us today. The wire spec is the permanent investment — designed for the protocol AMS wants to be when nothing is in the way (push-native, real-time, WebSocket, agents holding live connections). Edge wrappers are the disposable scaffolding that bridges the gap between that ideal and the runtimes available right now. As classes of runtimes evolve to hold the wire natively (e.g., when MCP gains first-class persistent streams, when agent frameworks add WebSocket-native peers), the corresponding wrapper shrinks toward zero and eventually deletes. The wire does not change. This is the inverse of the usual platform shape, where adapters become load-bearing forever and the protocol slowly calcifies around the worst constraints of the loudest runtime. Keeping the wire as the permanent investment and treating wrappers as explicitly throwaway is what prevents that calcification.
